summary: Interactive AI session for issue creation, structured JSON storage
reasoning: Humans are bad at writing structured data, AIs are bad at parsing unstructured data. The creation session lets the user speak naturally while the AI extracts structure (context, acceptance_criteria, constraints). The Foreman reads clean JSON — no markdown conventions or heuristic parsing. --quick flag for trivial issues.

summary: Single approval for deft work --loop
reasoning: User approves the first job's plan to verify Deft's approach. Subsequent jobs auto-approve. Balances oversight with autonomy — user doesn't need to babysit but gets a chance to course-correct early.

summary: 90-day compaction for closed issues
reasoning: Keep the JSONL file focused on active work. Git history preserves full record. 90 days is long enough to reference recent completions, short enough to prevent unbounded growth. Configurable via issues.compaction_days.

summary: Build native issue tracker instead of integrating Seeds/Beads
reasoning: Seeds validates JSONL+git approach but is Bun/TypeScript — shelling out adds runtime dependency. Deft already has JSONL+GenServer+ETS patterns from Site Log. Native Elixir implementation fits OTP architecture. Take concepts (ready queue, hash IDs, merge=union, atomic writes) but implement in-house.

summary: JSONL+git over Dolt or SQLite for issue storage
reasoning: Dolt is heavyweight (Go binary dependency) and solves distributed sync Deft doesn't need. SQLite adds a dependency for sequential access patterns. JSONL is consistent with Site Log and sessions, human-readable, git-diffable. merge=union gitattribute handles branch merges. Scale is small (tens-hundreds of issues).

summary: Minimal v0.1: no labels, no assignees, no templates
reasoning: Priority + dependencies cover the core need (what to work on next). Labels, assignees, and templates add complexity not justified until issue count grows or multi-agent assignment is needed. All are additive — easy to add later without breaking changes.

summary: Chunk-based decomposition, not file-based
reasoning: Real work has overlapping files. Foreman plans deliverables (1-3 Leads), not implementation steps. Dependency DAG + git worktrees handle integration. File ownership was too rigid.

summary: Runners are inline loops, different from Leads/Foreman
reasoning: Tasks can't own supervision subtrees. Runners making 3-10 LLM calls don't need gen_statem or OM. Asymmetry between agent tiers is acceptable.

summary: Lead actively steers Runners, is the memory bridge
reasoning: Lead is more trustworthy than OM for Runner context. Lead reads Runner output, evaluates, course-corrects. Pair-programming manager model.

summary: Worktree per Lead, conflict resolver merges
reasoning: Each Lead gets isolated worktree. Whoever has the conflict resolves it at merge time. Foreman merges in dependency order.

summary: Partial dependency unblocking via interface contracts
reasoning: Lead B doesn't wait for all of Lead A — just needs the specific contract (API shape). Foreman watches Site Log for contract entries and unblocks immediately.

summary: Remove suggested-response from Observer output
reasoning: Cheap model should not steer expensive Actor. Dynamic continuation hint provides continuity without the steering risk.

summary: Keep 30k first-activation threshold
reasoning: Lowering to 8-10k for short sessions not worth the extra Observer cost. OM is for long sessions.

summary: Observation sectioning promoted to requirement
reasoning: 10-agent review consensus. Named sections (Current State, User Preferences, Files & Architecture, Decisions, Session History) improve LLM attention, user readability, and Reflector compression. Current State at top gets highest attention weight.

summary: Keep Breeze for TUI despite risk
reasoning: User wants the LiveView-style API. Mitigation: build a streaming proof-of-concept early to validate performance. Fallback to Termite + custom rendering if it fails.

summary: No security/permission layer in v0.1
reasoning: User decision: focus on core functionality first. Tool permissions, sandboxing, secret redaction deferred to future spec.

summary: YAML for config format, not TOML
reasoning: TOML has poor Elixir ecosystem support (unmaintained libraries). YAML via yaml_elixir is actively maintained. JSON also acceptable.

summary: Burrito from day 1 for single binary
reasoning: Single binary distribution is a hard requirement. Accept the build complexity from the start rather than deferring.

summary: 40k reflection threshold validated by Mastra testing
reasoning: Mastra has empirical data showing 40k observation tokens work well with LLM attention. Context Expert recommended lowering to 20-25k, but real-world testing overrides theoretical concern.

summary: Replaced Observer/Reflector GenServers with Task-based architecture
reasoning: 10-agent review identified that long-running LLM calls in GenServers is an OTP anti-pattern (blocks the process, makes it unresponsive). Tasks spawned by State are simpler, cancellable, and don't require persistent processes.

summary: Per-session scope only for v0.1, cross-session deferred
reasoning: Cross-session memory (Mastra's resource scope) requires merging observations across threads and adds complexity around deduplication and conflict resolution. Per-session is the core value prop and sufficient for v0.1.

summary: Character-based token estimation over tokenizer dependency
reasoning: A real tokenizer (tiktoken) adds a NIF or large dependency. The 4:1 char/token ratio is well-known and sufficient for threshold decisions. Calibration from actual provider usage reports improves accuracy over time without adding deps.

summary: No embeddings or retrieval — always-present observations
reasoning: Retrieval-augmented memory requires the agent to know what it doesn't know. OM takes the opposite approach: everything observed is always injected. Token cost is managed by two-level compression (observation + reflection), not by selective retrieval. This matches Mastra's proven design.

summary: Named the project Deft
reasoning: Short, memorable, not an existing CLI command on Linux/macOS. Fits naming style of user's other projects (moto, specd). No major GitHub/npm/hex conflicts. Emacs has an elpa-deft package but it does not install a CLI binary.

summary: Chose Elixir/OTP over Pi SDK wrapper or TypeScript
reasoning: User wants stability, tailored flows, and natural concurrency for OM. Elixir's process model maps perfectly to Observer/Reflector as independent processes. Pi SDK wrapper would create an upstream dependency risk the user wants to avoid.

summary: Chose Breeze (Termite stack) for TUI over raw ANSI or other frameworks
reasoning: Breeze provides LiveView-style API that Elixir developers know, built-in components for scrolling/layout, and is actively maintained by a Phoenix core team member. Drafter and TermUI are alternatives but less proven. Raw ANSI is too much work for a proper chat interface.

summary: Chose gen_statem over GenServer for agent loop
reasoning: Agent loop has distinct states (idle, calling, streaming, executing_tools) with explicit transitions. gen_statem makes invalid transitions impossible at the framework level. Pattern from Opal validated this approach.

summary: Chose JSONL over SQLite for session persistence
reasoning: Sessions are read linearly (rebuild from start to end), append-only during operation, and human-readable for debugging. SQLite would add a dependency and is overkill for sequential access patterns.

summary: Scoped out MCP, sub-agents, and IDE integrations to future phases
reasoning: Core value is OM + stability. MCP and sub-agents add complexity without contributing to the differentiator. IDE integrations are a separate concern entirely.

summary: Learned from Opal but chose not to depend on it
reasoning: Opal validates Elixir/OTP works for coding agents (gen_statem loop, supervised tools, SSE streaming). But it's Copilot-only, hobby project, single author, very new — the exact instability risk user wants to avoid. Patterns are reusable without the dependency.

summary: Created harness as foundation spec, OM as separate spec
reasoning: Harness (agent loop, tools, TUI, sessions) and OM (observer, reflector, context injection) are distinct concerns with a clean process boundary between them. Separate specs allow independent iteration and implementation.
